title: WeakSet
tldr: WeakSet is a collection of objects only (no primitives), where each object can appear at most once.
Objects are held weakly — if no other references exist, the object is eligible for garbage collection.
WeakSet has no size property, no iteration methods, and only three methods: add(), has(), delete().
Primary use cases: tagging or marking objects without memory leaks (e.g., marking visited DOM nodes).
laymanDefinition: Imagine a guest list at an exclusive party. The bouncer has a mental list of VIPs, but doesn't write anything down — he just recognizes them. Once a VIP leaves the party (no longer around), the bouncer automatically forgets them. You can ask 'Is this person a VIP?' and the bouncer checks. But you can't ask 'How many VIPs are here?' or 'List all VIPs.' WeakSet is exactly this — a way to mark objects as part of a set, with automatic cleanup when the objects disappear.

deepDive.heading: WeakSet Fundamentals — Weak Object References
deepDive.text: WeakSet holds weak references to its objects. Adding an object to a WeakSet does not prevent its garbage collection. When all other references to the object are gone, the object is removed from the WeakSet automatically. This makes WeakSet ideal for tracking or tagging objects without interfering with their lifecycle.

deepDive.heading: Limited API — By Design
deepDive.text: WeakSet has only three methods: add(obj), has(obj), and delete(obj). There is no size, no clear(), no iteration (forEach, keys, values, entries). The API is intentionally minimal because objects can be removed by GC at any time, making iteration unreliable. You can only check if a specific object is in the set.

deepDive.heading: WeakSet vs Set
list: Values: Set accepts any type. WeakSet only accepts objects.
References: Set holds strong references. WeakSet holds weak references.
Iteration: Set is iterable (forEach, keys, values, entries). WeakSet is not iterable.
Size: Set has size property. WeakSet does not.
Use cases: Set for general-purpose unique collections. WeakSet for object tagging and lifecycle tracking.

deepDive.heading: WeakSet vs WeakMap — When to Use Each
deepDive.text: Use WeakSet when you only need to track whether an object is in the collection (membership testing). Use WeakMap when you need to associate data with an object. WeakSet is simpler — think of it as a 'tag set' for objects. WeakMap is for key-value associations. Both provide the same weak-reference benefit of automatic cleanup.

interviewAnswer: WeakSet is a collection of objects held by weak reference, allowing garbage collection when no other references exist. It has three methods: add(obj), has(obj), delete(obj). It is not iterable and has no size property. Primary use cases: marking/tagging objects (e.g., visited DOM nodes, processed items), tracking object membership without preventing GC, implementing safe object registries, and detecting cycles in object graphs. The main advantage over Set is automatic cleanup — objects are removed from the WeakSet when they are garbage collected, preventing memory leaks.

interviewQuestions.question: What is a WeakSet?
interviewQuestions.answer: A WeakSet is a collection of objects (only objects) held by weak reference. Objects are unique within the set and auto-removed when GC'd.

interviewQuestions.question: What types can be added to a WeakSet?
interviewQuestions.answer: Only objects. Adding primitive values (numbers, strings, booleans) throws a TypeError.

interviewQuestions.question: What methods does WeakSet have?
interviewQuestions.answer: add(obj), has(obj), and delete(obj). That is the complete API — no size, clear, or iteration methods.

interviewQuestions.question: Why is WeakSet not iterable?
interviewQuestions.answer: Objects can be garbage collected at any time, making iteration unreliable. The limited API ensures consistent behavior despite weak references.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between Set and WeakSet?
interviewQuestions.answer: Set holds strong references (prevents GC) and accepts any value type. WeakSet holds weak references, only accepts objects, and is not iterable.

interviewQuestions.question: What are common use cases for WeakSet?
interviewQuestions.answer: 1) Tracking visited/marked objects without memory leaks. 2) Cycle detection in object graphs. 3) Tagging DOM elements. 4) Object deduplication with auto-cleanup.

interviewQuestions.question: How does WeakSet prevent memory leaks?
interviewQuestions.answer: WeakSet holds weak references, so adding an object to a WeakSet doesn't prevent GC. When the object is no longer referenced, the WeakSet entry auto-clears.

interviewQuestions.question: Can you check if an object is in a WeakSet?
interviewQuestions.answer: Yes, using ws.has(obj). This is the primary operation — membership testing.

interviewQuestions.question: What happens to WeakSet entries when objects are garbage collected?
interviewQuestions.answer: They are automatically removed. has() returns false for objects that have been GC'd.

interviewQuestions.question: When would you choose WeakSet over WeakMap?
interviewQuestions.answer: When you only need to track membership (is this object in the set?), not associate additional data. WeakSet is simpler when no values are needed.

codeExamples.title: WeakSet Basics
codeExamples.description: WeakSet only accepts objects. Membership is reference-based, not value-based. Only three methods: add, has, delete.

codeExamples.title: Object Tagging with WeakSet
codeExamples.description: WeakSet is ideal for tagging objects as 'processed,' 'visited,' or 'cached' without modifying the objects or leaking memory.

codeExamples.title: DOM Node Tracking with WeakSet
codeExamples.useCase: Tracking DOM elements without memory leaks
codeExamples.description: WeakSet is useful for tracking DOM elements and component initialization states without causing memory leaks.

codeExamples.title: WeakSet vs Set — Memory Leak Comparison
codeExamples.useCase: Understanding when to use WeakSet over Set
codeExamples.description: Set holds strong references (can cause memory leaks). WeakSet holds weak references (auto-cleanup). Choose based on your lifecycle needs.

codeExamples.title: Practical WeakSet Patterns
codeExamples.description: WeakSet is useful for deduplication, object registries, feature flags, and plugin systems where automatic cleanup is desired.

mcqQuestions.question: What types can be added to a WeakSet?
mcqQuestions.explanation: WeakSet only accepts objects. Adding primitives (numbers, strings, booleans) throws a TypeError.

mcqQuestions.question: What methods does WeakSet have?
mcqQuestions.explanation: WeakSet has exactly three methods: add(), has(), and delete(). No clear(), no size, no iteration.

mcqQuestions.question: Why does WeakSet not have a size property?
options: Objects can be GC'd at any time, making size unreliable
It was omitted from the spec
mcqQuestions.explanation: WeakSet entries can be garbage collected at any time, so the size would be unpredictable.

mcqQuestions.question: Is WeakSet iterable?
options: No, it has no iteration methods
mcqQuestions.explanation: WeakSet has no iteration methods and cannot be used with for...of or forEach.

mcqQuestions.question: What is the primary advantage of WeakSet over Set?
options: Automatic cleanup when objects are garbage collected
mcqQuestions.explanation: WeakSet holds weak references, so entries are automatically removed when objects are garbage collected.

mcqQuestions.question: What is a common use case for WeakSet?
options: Marking objects as visited without memory leaks
mcqQuestions.explanation: WeakSet is ideal for tagging/marking objects (like visited, processed, initialized) without preventing garbage collection.

mcqQuestions.question: Can you check if an object is in a WeakSet?
mcqQuestions.explanation: has(obj) is the membership test. It returns true if the object is in the WeakSet.

mcqQuestions.question: What is the difference between WeakSet and WeakMap?
options: WeakSet tracks membership only; WeakMap associates values with keys
WeakSet is iterable; WeakMap is not
WeakSet allows primitives; WeakMap does not
mcqQuestions.explanation: WeakSet is for tagging objects (membership testing). WeakMap is for associating data values with object keys.

mcqQuestions.question: What does ws.delete(obj) return?
options: true if the object was in the set, false otherwise
mcqQuestions.explanation: delete() returns true if the object was present and removed, false if it was not found.

mcqQuestions.question: When would you choose WeakSet over a regular Set?
options: When you need to iterate over all values
When you need automatic cleanup of objects that are no longer referenced
When you need to know the number of entries
mcqQuestions.explanation: Use WeakSet when objects in the set should not be prevented from garbage collection. Use Set when you need iteration or size.
